quotes.quote: Data is made powerful by context. The more context you collect, the more powerful it becomes.
quotes.tone: urgent and persuasive, with dry humor
quotes.summary: Charity argues that the root cause of observability failures isn't culture or tooling but a fundamental data architecture problem: the 'three pillars' model (metrics, logs, traces) destroys the relational context that makes telemetry data exponentially more powerful. As agentic AI workflows demand increasingly precise production validation, fragmented telemetry siloes become not just suboptimal but a critical bottleneck — AI agents are already abandoning three-pillars data in favor of richer, context-intact signals.
quotes.key_insight: The three pillars observability model is not just inefficient but actively destructive — it eliminates context at write time, and no amount of AI-powered joining can restore what was never preserved, which makes it incompatible with the precision demands of agentic software development.

quotes.quote: By spinning your telemetry out into siloes based on signal type, the three pillars model ends up destroying the most valuable part of your data: its relational seams.

quotes.quote: Don't wait for it to be forced on you. Swim out to meet it.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors reflects on how her views on AI have shifted dramatically in the year since she co-keynoted SRECon25, where she urged skeptical SREs to engage with AI without being reflexively antagonistic. She now believes the center of gravity has fully shifted to AI/agentic workflows and advocates for engineers to proactively embrace this change rather than wait for it to be forced on them. SREs in particular, with their outcome-orientation and experience building guardrails, are well-positioned to lead in this new era.
quotes.key_insight: SREs' career-long focus on outcomes over craft makes them uniquely positioned to lead — not resist — the AI transition, but only if they proactively engage rather than wait for change to be forced on them.

quotes.quote: I was writing tactical advice for teams who were surviving in a strategic vacuum.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors describes how she had to scrap and rewrite an entire section of the second edition of Observability Engineering after realizing she was writing tactical advice for teams operating in a strategic vacuum. Feedback from readers revealed that the real problems weren't technical implementation challenges but deeply dysfunctional organizational buying processes and a lack of shared understanding between engineering and leadership about what observability even is and why it matters.
quotes.key_insight: The biggest barrier to effective observability isn't technical implementation but the lack of shared strategic alignment between engineering teams and organizational leadership about what problem they're actually trying to solve.

quotes.quote: When we started the book in 2018, Honeycomb was the only observability company, and our definition of observability—high cardinality, high dimensionality, explorability—was the only definition. By the time the book came out in 2021, everyone was rebranding their products as observability, Gartner had waded into the fray.. it was a mess.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors announces the near-completion of the second edition of 'Observability Engineering,' which is almost twice as long as the first despite Martin Fowler's advice to make it shorter. The new edition features 90% new material, a clearer mission focused on software engineers, and contributions from a diverse group of industry practitioners, reflecting how much the observability landscape has changed since 2018.
quotes.key_insight: The observability landscape has transformed so dramatically since 2018—with OpenTelemetry winning, AI reshaping workflows, and the market co-opting the term—that a near-complete rewrite of the foundational book was necessary to reflect what observability actually means and requires today.

quotes.quote: Operations is not a dirty word, a synonym for toil, or a title for people who can't write code. May those who shit on ops get the operational outcomes they deserve.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that the software industry has wrongly turned 'operations' into a dirty word, when ops is actually a vital separation of concerns — not a synonym for toil or a label for people who can't code. She traces this cultural rot partly to Google's SRE movement conflating 'writes code' with 'good' and 'does things by hand' with 'bad,' and calls for reclaiming operations as a term of pride to improve actual operational outcomes.
quotes.key_insight: The software industry's cultural contempt for 'operations' is both cause and consequence of poor operational outcomes — reclaiming ops as a term of pride is a prerequisite for building operational excellence.

quotes.quote: The one thing that does get my knickers in a twist is when people adopt a holier-than-thou posture towards their Friday deploy freezes. Like they're doing it because they Care About People and it's the Right Thing To Do and some sort of grand moral gesture. Dude, it's a fucking hack. Just admit it.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors clarifies her stance on Friday deploy freezes, arguing they're a pragmatic workaround rather than a virtue to be celebrated. She offers two practical alternatives: freeze merges instead of deploys to prevent dangerous change buildup, and keep running the deploy pipeline without new code to avoid breakage from prolonged inaction.
quotes.key_insight: Deploy freezes are a practical hack for teams lacking observability confidence, not a virtue — and if you must freeze, freeze merges instead of deploys to avoid a dangerous January pile-up of untested changes.

quotes.quote: But the existence of froth doesn't disprove the existence of value.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that 2025 marked the tipping point where AI shifted from experimental satellite technology to mainstream foundational tech in developer tools, drawing a direct parallel to how cloud computing made the same transition around 2010. She acknowledges the bubble and hype but insists that froth doesn't disprove underlying value, and urges skeptics to engage rather than dismiss.
quotes.key_insight: AI has crossed the same inflection point cloud computing did around 2010 — it's no longer experimental but foundational — and skeptics must engage with it substantively rather than dismiss it as hype.

quotes.quote: I have a job to do, and I haven't been doing it.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors explains her decision to move from WordPress to Substack despite criticism about the platform's content moderation policies. She argues that she's been neglecting a core part of her job as Honeycomb's CTO by going dark on social media, and needs a lower-friction platform to re-engage with the mainstream tech community. She acknowledges the ethical concerns but concludes Substack is far from being a platform that solely exists to support extremism.
quotes.key_insight: Sometimes the most responsible professional choice is to prioritize reaching your audience over platform purity, especially when silence means failing to do your job.

quotes.quote: It is not enough to have interesting ideas and say them once, or write them down in a single book. You need to be out there mixing it up with the community every day, or at least every week.

quotes.key_insight: Publishing friction kills writing output, and platform migration — even after a decade — is worth it to remove barriers between ideas and audience.

quotes.quote: If your 'observability' tooling doesn't help you understand the quality of your product from the customer's perspective, EACH customer's perspective, it isn't fucking observability. It's just monitoring dressed up in marketing dollars.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that the term 'observability' has been co-opted by vendors and executives who use it to describe basic monitoring tools, while the actual problem observability was meant to solve — understanding service quality from each individual customer's perspective — remains unaddressed by most 'observability' products. She coins the term 'o11ywashing' (analogous to 'cloudwashing') to describe this phenomenon and argues Honeycomb needs to better communicate observability's true value to executives, not just engineers.
quotes.key_insight: The term 'observability' has been hollowed out by vendors and executives to mean basic monitoring, and fixing this requires communicating observability's true value — understanding per-customer service quality as a systems problem — directly to engineering executives in business terms, not just to engineers in technical ones.

She coins the term 'o11ywashing' (analogous to 'cloudwashing') to describe this phenomenon and argues Honeycomb needs to better communicate observability's true value to executives, not just engineers.
quotes.key_insight: The term 'observability' has been hollowed out by vendors and executives to mean basic monitoring, and fixing this requires communicating observability's true value — understanding per-customer service quality as a systems problem — directly to engineering executives in business terms, not just to engineers in technical ones.

quotes.quote: There are no pillars. I think the pillars are a fucking lie, dude. I think the language of pillars does a lot of work to keep good engineers trapped inside a mental model from the 1980s, paying outrageous sums of money for tooling that can't keep up with the chaos and complexity of modern systems.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that the 'three pillars of observability' (metrics, logs, traces) is a marketing construct, not a technical one, used by vendors to justify selling siloed products at inflated prices. She advocates for a unified storage model (o11y 2.0) where all telemetry signals are stored together in one database, enabling seamless zoom-in/zoom-out debugging instead of bunny-hopping between disconnected tools. The piece also addresses misconceptions about OpenTelemetry reinforcing the pillars model, arguing it actually unifies signals through shared distributed context.
quotes.key_insight: The 'pillars of observability' is a vendor marketing construct that justifies expensive siloed tooling; the technically superior approach is unified storage where all telemetry signals live together, enabling seamless debugging through zoom-in/zoom-out rather than hopping between disconnected data stores.

quotes.quote: When a vendor starts talking about pillars, it means they are gearing up to sell you something: another type of signal, siloed off from all the other signals you send them. Your cost multiplier is about to increment again.

quotes.key_insight: Vendor engineering — the art of evaluating, buying, and managing software tools — is an underrated, high-leverage skill that lets staff+ engineers develop executive-level strategic thinking while remaining individual contributors.

quotes.quote: Rails HTTP processing is built on a philosophy of 'be liberal in what you accept'. So developers end up inadvertently sending API requests that are undocumented or even non-RFC compliant … but Rails middleware cleans them up and handles it fine.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors resurrects a lost blog post from her Parse days documenting the two-year rewrite of Parse's API from Ruby on Rails to Go. The migration was driven by Rails' one-process-per-request model failing to scale during hockey-stick growth, and the team used production traffic shadowing to catch undocumented Rails behaviors. The experience of diffing individual API responses field-by-field during the migration directly inspired what would become Honeycomb's approach to observability.
quotes.key_insight: The grueling process of migrating Parse's API from Ruby to Go — and the production traffic diffing tools required to do it safely — directly seeded the observability philosophy that became Honeycomb.

quotes.quote: Once you've used a tool like that, you're hooked.. you can't possibly go back to metrics and aggregates. The rest, as they say, is history.

quotes.quote: Creation is harder than critique — so, so, so much harder. And reality resists easy answers.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors reflects on her unconventional path from a fundamentalist homeschooled upbringing in rural Idaho to becoming a tech company founder, arguing that work can be a source of liberation, meaning, and personal growth rather than mere exploitation. She makes the case that people with strong ethics should pursue business and institutional power rather than ceding it to those without principles, and that the second half of a career should be oriented around purpose and building things of enduring value.
quotes.key_insight: People with strong ethics should actively pursue power and build institutions rather than ceding business and technology to those without principles, because creation and institutional stewardship — not critique — is what brings ideals to life.

quotes.quote: The most quietly radical, rebellious thing I can possibly do is to be an institutionalist, someone who builds instead of performatively tearing it all down.

quotes.quote: The smallest unit of software ownership and delivery is the engineering team.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that the tech industry's obsession with '10x engineers' is misguided because individual engineers don't own software—teams do. She makes the case that truly great engineering organizations are ones where normal, workaday engineers can consistently ship code and have business impact, rather than orgs that depend on a few brilliant individuals. The path to high performance is building better sociotechnical systems (fast deploys, observability, guard rails) that enable normal people to do great work.
quotes.key_insight: The mark of a great engineering organization isn't hiring the most brilliant individuals, but building sociotechnical systems where normal engineers can consistently ship, learn, and move the business forward.

quotes.quote: Any asshole can build an org where the most experienced, brilliant engineers in the world can build product and make progress. That is not hard. And putting all the spotlight on individual ability has a way of letting your leaders off the hook for doing their jobs.

She advises trusting early impressions and learning from bad fits rather than waiting for things to improve.
quotes.key_insight: Trust your early gut feeling about a new job — especially as a manager, where alignment with the company isn't optional but is the fundamental nature of the work itself.

quotes.quote: The strongest feeling I have about my gender is apathy/discomfort/irritation. Maybe that will change someday, maybe it won't, but I resent being forced to pick a side and make some kind of public declaration when I'm just trying to do my goddamn job.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues against mandating pronoun sharing at work while supporting the normalization and safety of voluntary disclosure. Drawing from her own experience as a queer person with complicated feelings about gender identity, she explains how forced pronoun sharing can backfire by pressuring people who are questioning or transitioning, priming gender bias, and eliminating useful signal about genuine allyship.
quotes.key_insight: Well-intentioned pronoun mandates at work can backfire by pressuring vulnerable people, activating gender bias, and destroying the very social signals that help queer people identify genuine allies.

quotes.quote: Ever since then, I've met wave after wave of people in this industry who are convinced they know how to sift "good" talent from "bad" via easily detected heuristics. They're mostly bullshit.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors reflects on how she and her college-dropout peers in early Silicon Valley developed an unfounded contempt for CS degree holders, using cherry-picked evidence to justify their own paths. She draws a broader lesson about how people in tech construct self-serving heuristics to validate their belonging, and argues for humility given the enormous role of luck and timing in tech careers.
quotes.key_insight: The heuristics tech workers use to judge talent are usually self-serving rationalizations that reinforce their own backgrounds, and the honest response to career success in tech is humility about how much luck and timing mattered.

quotes.quote: I have retained an abiding skepticism of heuristics that serve to reinforce existing power structures, or ones that just so happen to overlap with the background of the holder of said heuristics.

quotes.quote: In retrospect, I now realize that I was doing things like 'outbounding' and 'product marketing' and 'category creation', but at the time it felt more like oxygen.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors reflects on how Twitter was essential to both Honeycomb's growth and her personal creative process, serving as a dopamine source for her undiagnosed ADHD. With social media fragmented and her old writing workflow broken, she commits to a three-month experiment of writing shorter, faster blog posts weekly — learning to ship without the crutch of Twitter threads or deadline-driven panic.
quotes.key_insight: The writing that endures is the writing you ship in durable form, not the writing that feeds the dopamine cycle of social media — and learning to ship without those crutches requires deliberately embracing imperfection.

quotes.quote: The fatal flaw of the multiple pillars model is that your levers of control consist of deleting your most valuable data: context and detail.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors responds to Matt Klein's claim that bitdrift's ring buffer model deserves the 'Observability 3.0' label, while reflecting on the industry's shift from multiple pillars to unified storage models. She argues that while Klein raises valid points about cost control, his mobile-first approach of not sending telemetry by default doesn't translate well to complex, multi-tenant distributed systems that require real-time debugging. The industry is moving toward a data lake model with telemetry pipelines, tiered storage, and tail-based sampling as the next evolution of cost control.
quotes.key_insight: The observability industry is converging on unified storage models with telemetry pipelines and tail-based sampling as cost control levers, but the real value lies in workflows and context, not just reducing spend.

quotes.quote: The problem isn't the fact that companies talk about their values, it's that they treat it like a branding exercise instead of an accountability mechanism.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that corporate DEI programs were always ineffective symbolic gestures, but the ideals underlying them — inclusion, equity, and counteracting bias — are essential to building genuinely meritocratic, high-performing teams. She contends that real cultural change came from hearts and minds being changed through storytelling and persuasion in the 2010s, not from corporate mandates, and that the best way to spread these values is to build successful companies that embody them.
quotes.key_insight: True meritocracy demands active inclusion and bias counteraction — not corporate DEI theater — and the most powerful way to spread these values is to build winning companies that authentically embody them.

quotes.quote: But Brian Chesky created the fucking problems, by being bad at running the business! There is actually no shame in this! He is right: being a CEO is fucking hard. It does not come naturally. Nobody is born good at it.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors dissects Brian Chesky's 'founder mode' philosophy and hiring advice, acknowledging solid principles buried within but sharply criticizing his complete lack of accountability for Airbnb's organizational failures, his contemptuous treatment of employees, and the dangerous CEO-centric mythology that Silicon Valley continues to propagate. She extracts actionable lessons on hiring, leadership presence, and organizational efficiency while arguing that there are many valid architectural approaches to running a company.
quotes.key_insight: The 'founder mode' philosophy contains valid leadership principles about staying engaged with details and hiring for strengths, but its dangerous packaging as CEO-centric mythology ignores that organizational dysfunction is a systems problem driven by leadership choices, not proof that everyone else is mediocre.

quotes.quote: Observability 1.0 is a dinner knife; 2.0 is a scalpel.
quotes.summary: The fundamental distinction between observability 1.0 and 2.0 is not a marketing buzzword but a technical one: 1.0 scatters telemetry across multiple pillars, tools, and formats (many sources of truth), while 2.0 stores everything as wide structured log events in a single source of truth. Everything else—better developer workflows, sustainable cost models, high-cardinality exploration—flows as a consequence from this one architectural difference.
quotes.key_insight: The entire observability 1.0 vs 2.0 distinction reduces to one technical question—whether your telemetry is stored once as wide structured events (single source of truth) or scattered across multiple pillars and tools—and every other advantage of 2.0 flows as a consequence of that single architectural choice.

quotes.quote: The harder your employer is affirmatively working to do right by you, the fewer heroics they will be willing or able to do to retain you.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that companies should build fair, transparent compensation systems and invest in ongoing career development rather than relying on heroic counter-offers when employees threaten to leave. She contends that healthy turnover is natural, that managers should proactively identify superstars and single points of failure, and that exceptions to compensation frameworks—while occasionally necessary—compound into cultural damage and cynicism.
quotes.key_insight: Sustainable employee retention comes from building fair, transparent systems for compensation and career development—not from heroic counter-offers that reward brinksmanship and erode organizational trust.

quotes.quote: You cannot simultaneously store your data across both multiple pillars and a single source of truth. Incompatible. Breaking change. O11y 1.0, meet O11y 2.0.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that observability tooling needs a version bump: 'Observability 1.0' (metrics, logs, traces as separate pillars) is fundamentally incompatible with 'Observability 2.0' (wide, structured log events as a single source of truth). She contends that metrics-based tools are increasingly expensive and inadequate for understanding complex modern systems, while structured event-based approaches offer superior debugging, cost efficiency, and developer experience.
quotes.key_insight: The shift from metrics-based observability to wide structured log events isn't an incremental improvement but a breaking change that unlocks fundamentally different — and vastly simpler — ways of understanding, debugging, and developing complex software systems.

quotes.quote: Writing code is the easiest part of software engineering, and it's getting easier by the day. The hard parts are what you do with that code—operating it, understanding it, extending it, and governing it over its entire lifecycle.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that the tech industry's belief that generative AI can replace junior engineers is dangerously misguided, because writing code is the easiest part of software engineering while the hard parts—operating, understanding, and managing complex sociotechnical systems—require human judgment built through years of apprenticeship. By refusing to hire and train junior engineers, companies are cannibalizing their own future pipeline of senior engineers.
quotes.key_insight: Generative AI makes the already-easy part of software engineering (writing code) even easier, but does nothing for the hard parts (managing complex systems), so using it as justification to stop hiring junior engineers is a catastrophic mistake that will destroy the industry's pipeline of future senior talent.

quotes.quote: For an alarming number of people, the value they get actually decreases as their bill goes up.
quotes.summary: The traditional 'three pillars' approach to observability (metrics, logs, traces) creates a cost crisis where organizations pay to store the same telemetry data multiple times across different tools, with costs growing several times faster than traffic while the value derived actually decreases. Charity argues that 'observability 2.0' built on arbitrarily-wide structured log events as a single source of truth fundamentally changes the cost-value equation, making telemetry more valuable as you add more data rather than less.
quotes.key_insight: The observability cost crisis isn't primarily about the dollar amount on the invoice—it's that the traditional multi-pillar approach structurally guarantees that costs and value move in opposite directions, while a single-source-of-truth approach aligns them.

quotes.quote: When there is no explicit structure or hierarchy, the result is not freedom and egalitarianism, it's 'informal, unacknowledged, and unaccountable leadership.'
quotes.summary: Charity argues that engineering managers are essential even at startups because hierarchy is a property of self-organizing systems that reduces coordination costs and information overload. She makes the case through systems theory (managers coordinate between teams and reduce context switching) and through pragmatism (most orgs have managers because the model is well-understood, and manager-free experiments have repeatedly failed).
quotes.key_insight: Engineering managers are not overhead but a necessary abstraction layer in self-organizing systems — they reduce coordination costs, prevent information overload, and ensure that building the organization is someone's primary job rather than no one's.

quotes.summary: When trust is low in a working relationship, every communication gets interpreted in the worst possible light, creating a death spiral. The post offers concrete tactical advice for rebuilding trust through overcommunication, tentative language, engineered positive interactions, and explicitly stating intent — drawn from the author's experience rebuilding her fractured cofounder relationship.
quotes.key_insight: Rebuilding trust requires deliberately overcommunicating positive intent and engineering frequent low-stakes positive interactions, because in a low-trust environment every neutral statement will be read as an attack.

quotes.quote: The system can't survive if every manager is gaming the rules on behalf of their team. The system only works if every manager is playing fair.
quotes.summary: New managers often over-optimize for being liked by their direct reports while neglecting the broader organizational responsibilities of the role. Helicopter management—coddling people, preventing failure, and over-identifying with your team—can harm both the organization and the individuals you're trying to help. Great management requires balancing care for your team with strategic direction, stakeholder management, and serving the organizational system fairly.
quotes.key_insight: The most common rookie manager mistake is over-optimizing for your team's happiness and loyalty at the expense of organizational effectiveness, and the hardest lesson is that truly caring for people sometimes means letting them struggle.

quotes.quote: The Shrek ears were a brilliant piece of social jujitsu.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors recounts how Linden Lab's tradition of wearing Shrek ears after breaking production created a psychologically safe culture around failure long before 'blameless retrospectives' existed as a concept. The opt-in ritual transformed what could be shame into celebration, teaching engineers that failure is inevitable, constant, and even funny. While acknowledging the practice might not translate to modern workplaces, she argues it was a brilliant piece of social engineering that made transparency about mistakes infectious across the entire company.
quotes.key_insight: Workplace rituals that transform failure from shameful to celebratory are powerful tools for building psychologically safe engineering cultures, but they must evolve as our understanding of workplace dynamics matures.

quotes.quote: If culture means everything, then culture means nothing.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that companies should 'choose boring technology culture' by focusing on organizational health rather than cultural innovation. She distinguishes between formal organizational culture (which should be stable and load-bearing) and informal culture (which emerges naturally from people when the formal structure is healthy). The core thesis is that business success earns you innovation tokens, and most companies should spend those tokens sparingly on culture, instead focusing on executing well.
quotes.key_insight: Great workplace culture isn't something leaders manufacture — it emerges naturally when they focus on organizational health and business success, creating the stable foundation that frees people to bring their own joy and creativity.

quotes.quote: I built something because I cared.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors reflects on her journey from being an open-source zealot who dismissed software vendors to becoming one herself, and the karmic experience of having her expertise discounted because she's now 'selling something.' She offers practical advice for vendor-side engineers to maintain credibility by leading with bias disclosure, engaging competitors' strongest arguments, and being generous with expertise.
quotes.key_insight: The open-source world's reflexive contempt for software vendors is a self-limiting bias that ignores how much value paid software creates, and vendor-side engineers can maintain credibility by leading with transparency about their biases rather than hiding them.

She contends that architecture should be a core engineering skill embedded in teams, not a separate role that unbundles decision-making authority from responsibility for results. The post explores several healthier patterns including embedded architects, architecture working groups, and engineer-architect pendulum swings.
quotes.key_insight: Architecture is a core engineering skill that should be embedded in teams, not externalized into a separate role that severs the feedback loop between decision-making and living with the consequences.

quotes.quote: Deploying software is a terrible, horrible, no good, very bad way to go about the process of changing user-facing code.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that organizations should decouple deploys (building, testing, and rolling out code) from releases (changing user experience), because using the deploy process to control what users see is slow, flaky, and unreliable. Feature flags solve this by letting teams deploy code continuously while controlling user-visible changes independently, enabling progressive delivery and safer testing in production.
quotes.key_insight: Decoupling deploys from releases via feature flags transforms shipping from a high-stakes engineering event into a routine, low-risk operation that non-engineers can control.

quotes.summary: Charity Majors describes how Honeycomb organized their first all-hands offsite in three years, detailing their layered COVID safety approach including testing, CO2 monitors, outdoor spaces, and social distancing options. quotes.key_insight: Distributed companies must treat in-person gatherings as essential infrastructure, not perks, and can do so responsibly by taking a layered approach to safety and being honest about what worked and what didn't.

quotes.quote: Every achievement has a denominator.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that engineering leaders should be evaluated not by the size of their organizations, but by the ratio of impact to team size. She challenges the empire-building instinct in management, proposing that delivering outsized results with small, lean teams is a far better measure of leadership skill than simply accumulating headcount.
quotes.key_insight: The true measure of engineering leadership isn't how many people you manage, but how much impact you deliver per person — every achievement should be evaluated relative to the resources consumed to produce it.

quotes.quote: Bigness itself is costly. There's the cost of the engineers, managers, product and designers etc, of course. quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that the operations profession is bifurcating: infrastructure expertise is moving to cloud providers while a new discipline called platform engineering is emerging to help software engineers self-serve and own their code in production. quotes.key_insight: Platform engineering is the natural successor to DevOps, shifting the focus from managing infrastructure to enabling developers to self-serve and own their code in production through curated, product-like internal platforms.

quotes.quote: The "Big Lie" of hierarchy is that your organizational structure is a vertical tree from the CEO on down, where higher up is always better.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that organizational hierarchies become toxic when conflated with social dominance and status, pushing talented people like her friend Molly into management roles they don't actually want. She advocates for treating hierarchy as a neutral organizational data structure rather than a measure of human worth, and calls for radical transparency, distributed decision-making power, and career paths that let people follow intrinsic motivation rather than climbing ladders by default.
quotes.key_insight: Organizations should treat hierarchy as a neutral tool for coordination rather than a status ladder, and must actively distribute power and transparency to technical contributors so people choose roles based on curiosity and fulfillment rather than chasing status.

quotes.quote: Observability is about the unknown-unknowns. Which requires a fundamentally different mindset and toolchain.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that modern distributed systems require a fundamental shift from traditional monitoring and metrics to observability built on arbitrarily wide, structured raw events. She explains that as monoliths gave way to microservices, old debugging tools like printf and gdb became insufficient, and engineers must adopt structured events that capture rich context per request per service hop. The post lays out the technical requirements for true observability: structured, wide, raw, sampled events organized by request ID with trace awareness.
quotes.key_insight: True observability requires storing arbitrarily wide, structured raw events per request per service hop — not metrics, not unstructured logs, not pre-aggregated data — because only raw events let you ask questions you never anticipated.

quotes.quote: I can pretty much guarantee that none of the people you love working with realize just how much you value them, but they are acutely aware of all the ways they fall short or fail you.
quotes.summary: The post argues that people giving feedback should be mindful of the ratio of critique to affirmation they deliver, because recipients often don't know how valued they are and only remember the criticisms. Charity offers specific techniques for making positive feedback land—being specific, using the SBI framework for praise, grounding critique in overall reaction, and giving feedback that helps people grow.
quotes.key_insight: Before delivering critical feedback, consider whether the ratio of critique to affirmation you've given someone accurately represents how much you actually value them—because they almost certainly don't know.

quotes.quote: Writing code is like speaking a language: you use it or lose it. And just like with a language, the best way to freshen up is full immersion.
quotes.summary: A former CTO asking about returning to engineering should come back as a regular software engineer, not a staff+ role, and spend at least 6 months doing hands-on daily coding with a great team. The transition requires not just refreshing technical skills but re-acclimating to the rhythms of deep focus work, reconnecting with curiosity, and rebuilding empathy for production code — similar to how moving into management required learning an entirely different way of working.
quotes.key_insight: The best path back from CTO to engineer is full immersion in daily hands-on coding with a great team, starting fresh with new technologies rather than trying to resume where you left off, because rebuilding tactical skills and reconnecting with the emotional reality of shipping code matters more than preserving your title.

quotes.quote: It is engineering's job to own their code in production. It is management's job to make sure it doesn't suck.
quotes.summary: On-call pain is not an inevitable cost of owning code in production — it's a sociotechnical problem requiring both technical fixes (alert discipline, SLOs, proper tooling) and organizational solutions (management accountability, fair rotations, humane policies). Engineering owns their code, but management owns the responsibility to make on-call sustainable, and failure to do so is a five-alarm fire that should be fixed with urgency.
quotes.key_insight: On-call suffering is never just a technical problem — it's a management accountability problem, and the two-way pact between engineering ownership and management support must be enforced for on-call to be sustainable.

quotes.quote: You're never going to make a metrics tool like Prometheus or Datadog into an observability tool. You're just not. Observability is about unknown-unknowns, while metrics are a tool for known-unknowns.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors walks back her inflammatory anti-metrics rhetoric, clarifying that metrics aren't worthless—they're just the wrong tool for observability. She draws a clear line between 'your code' (which demands observability with rich, high-cardinality events) and 'other people's code' (third-party infrastructure where metrics and monitoring are the right fit).
quotes.key_insight: Metrics aren't bad—they're just scoped to aggregate system health and third-party infrastructure, while your own code demands the richer, event-level understanding that only observability provides.

quotes.quote: You are more likely to spend the rest of your career fending off management "opportunities" with a baseball bat than you are to wither away, pining for another shot.
quotes.summary: The post addresses two common anxieties people face when swinging between engineering and management roles: the fear of never getting another management opportunity, and the fear of being too rusty to return to engineering. Charity argues the first fear is misplaced since good managers are always in demand, while the second is more valid since technical skills genuinely erode over time. She advises engineers to be strategic about refreshing technical skills for both effectiveness and job security.
quotes.key_insight: The engineer-manager pendulum's two anxieties are asymmetric: good managers will always be recruited back into management, but technical skills genuinely decay, making it essential to strategically refresh them for both effectiveness and career optionality.

quotes.quote: Your labor is valuable — it is vital — and you should be scrutinizing them every bit as closely as they you.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors provides a comprehensive guide for job candidates to evaluate whether a company is healthy or toxic during the interview process. She argues that candidates hold more power than they realize and should rigorously scrutinize potential employers through backchannel research, probing questions about diversity, business health, team dynamics, and engineering practices. The post emphasizes that interviews are a two-way street and encourages people to leave quickly if a new job feels wrong.
quotes.key_insight: Job candidates should flip the interview power dynamic by rigorously evaluating companies through backchannels, probing questions about failure and diversity, and concrete signals like deploy frequency — and should leave without guilt if what they find doesn't match.

quotes.key_insight: True engineering excellence requires treating every business function as an equal partner, because without business success, even the best engineering culture becomes a cautionary tale.

quotes.quote: You can't just blindly throw 'more communication' at your teams. Too much communication can be just as much of a problem and a burden as too little.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that the phrase 'breaking down silos' is a meaningless cliché that substitutes for actual critical thinking about organizational communication problems. She contends that real solutions to collaboration problems are complex and specific, and people who use vague buzzphrases almost never have the hands-on experience of actually solving these problems.
quotes.key_insight: Organizational buzzphrases like 'breaking down silos' are red flags that signal someone lacks the specific, hard-won experience of actually diagnosing and solving complex communication and collaboration problems.

quotes.quote: When someone says they are 'breaking down silos', whether in an interview, a panel, or casual conversation, it tells me jack shit about what they actually did.

quotes.quote: We leap to conclusions with the wetware we have doing the best it can based on heuristics that feel objectively true, but are ultimately just emotional reactions based on past lived experience. And then we retroactively enshrine those goofy gut feelings with the language of noble motive and moral values.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors catalogs the many cognitive biases that lead engineering teams to adopt overly cautious deployment practices like 'no Friday deploys,' arguing that every common justification for slowing down deployments is materially false. She frames resistance to continuous deployment not as rational risk management but as emotional reactions retroactively dressed up in noble-sounding language.
quotes.key_insight: Resistance to frequent, automated deployments is driven not by legitimate technical concerns but by a catalog of cognitive biases that make inaction feel safer than action, when in reality speed and small changes are what make deploys safe.

quotes.quote: Slow is dangerous for software; speed is safety. The more frequently you ship code, the smaller the diffs you ship, the less dangerous each one actually becomes.

quotes.quote: Our industry has gotten very good at pressing operations engineers to get better at writing code, writing tests, and software engineering in general these past few years. Which is great! But we have not been nearly so good at pushing software engineers to level up their systems skills.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that software engineering interviews should include operations questions, just as ops/SRE interviews now include coding questions. She contends that operational excellence is everyone's responsibility, and that interviewing for ops knowledge both identifies engineers who will own their code in production and sets cultural expectations from day one.
quotes.key_insight: Including operations questions in software engineering interviews isn't just about evaluating technical breadth — it's a cultural signal that sets expectations about code ownership and operational responsibility from the very first interaction with a candidate.

quotes.quote: You need to balance out your software engineers with engineers who don't react to every problem by writing more code. You need engineers who write code begrudgingly, as a last resort. You'll find these priceless gems in ops and SRE.

quotes.quote: You should probably spend 20-30% of infra costs on observability.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that organizations should spend 20-30% of their infrastructure costs on observability, pushing back against the common instinct to minimize tooling spend. She makes the case that 'saving money' on observability by using free/open-source tools often costs far more in hidden engineering time, recruitment, and degraded developer experience.
quotes.key_insight: The true cost of observability is never just the vendor bill — organizations that cheap out on tooling pay far more in hidden engineering time, attrition, and degraded capability than they ever save.

quotes.quote: We aren't used to considering the price of our own time. We act like it's 'free' to just take an hour and spin something up … we don't count the cost of maintenance, context switching, and opportunity costs of not using the time to build something of business value.

quotes.quote: That's not debugging, that's pattern-matching. That's … eyeball racing.
quotes.summary: Static dashboards are poor debugging tools because they encourage pattern-matching against historical answers rather than methodical investigation of current problems. While useful as high-level system health overviews, dashboards strip away the connective tissue between events, making it impossible to correlate attributes or follow a trail of breadcrumbs to diagnose novel issues. The industry needs to move toward queryable, exploratory interfaces that let engineers interrogate their systems rather than scan pre-built graphs.
quotes.key_insight: Dashboards seduce engineers into pattern-matching against past incidents rather than methodically investigating present ones, and the industry needs to replace this habit with queryable, exploratory tools that support real debugging of unknown-unknowns.

quotes.quote: Management isn't a promotion, it's a change of career. And if you aren't up for resetting the dial to 'NOOB' for a couple years, with all of the ups and downs, anxieties and discombobulations, then you aren't up for management.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors maps out seven common career inflection points for engineering managers, arguing against rigid career planning and hierarchical ladder-climbing in favor of self-knowledge, preserving optionality, and following intrinsic motivation. She emphasizes that management is a career change (not a promotion), advocates for the engineer-manager pendulum, and urges people to prioritize doing work they genuinely love over chasing titles and status.
quotes.key_insight: Instead of planning careers around hierarchical advancement, engineers and managers should cultivate self-knowledge, preserve optionality by rotating between roles, and have the courage to choose work they genuinely love over titles and status.

quotes.summary: Charity Majors lays out a practical roadmap for engineering managers to develop their careers, arguing that growth should focus on craft mastery rather than climbing the org chart. She covers concrete skills from confrontation and public speaking to understanding power dynamics and optimizing sociotechnical systems, emphasizing that most managers were dropped into the role with little guidance.
quotes.key_insight: Engineering management career development should focus on mastering the craft itself — peer networks, confrontation skills, self-awareness, and sociotechnical thinking — rather than chasing promotions up the org chart.

quotes.quote: There is no correlation that I've observed between someone's engineering skills and management skills – no positive correlation, at any rate.
quotes.summary: When hiring an engineering manager, companies should always give internal candidates first consideration before looking externally, since effective management requires deep knowledge of the tech stack, existing relationships, and team trust. The post argues that management should be treated as a lateral career change rather than a promotion, and that the best engineer is rarely the right choice for manager.
quotes.key_insight: Engineering management should be treated as a lateral career change that prioritizes internal candidates with strong relationships and team trust, not as a promotion for your best technical performer.

quotes.quote: If you ARE competently performing your core responsibilities as a database engineer, and are performing them at the next level, then all your additional work for the company — on DEI, ERGs, mentoring, interviewing, etc — adds up to a MASSIVELY compelling body of work, and a powerful argument for promotion.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors responds to the backlash from her previous post about prioritizing your core job responsibilities, addressing objections about glue work, DEI labor, gendered dynamics, and management failures. She clarifies that while extracurricular work like ERGs and mentoring should factor into promotions, they cannot substitute for fulfilling the technical requirements you were hired for. She acknowledges her advice assumes a high-trust environment and encourages engineers to take ownership of career clarity even when managers fail them.
quotes.key_insight: Fulfilling your core job responsibilities is the foundation that makes all your other valuable contributions — glue work, DEI labor, mentoring — count toward career advancement rather than being seen as distractions from unfinished work.

She clarifies that while extracurricular work like ERGs and mentoring should factor into promotions, they cannot substitute for fulfilling the technical requirements you were hired for. She acknowledges her advice assumes a high-trust environment and encourages engineers to take ownership of career clarity even when managers fail them.
quotes.key_insight: Fulfilling your core job responsibilities is the foundation that makes all your other valuable contributions — glue work, DEI labor, mentoring — count toward career advancement rather than being seen as distractions from unfinished work.

quotes.quote: When someone isn't meeting the basic expectations for their core responsibilities, it doesn't matter how many other wonderful things they are doing. In fact, those things can become strikes *against* them.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that no amount of extracurricular contributions — interviewing, mentoring, speaking, community building — can compensate for failing to deliver on your core job responsibilities. She illustrates through a story how high-visibility side work actually becomes evidence against you when your primary deliverables are slipping. The post urges engineers to ruthlessly prioritize their 'One Job' first, and to honestly reassess whether they're in the right role if they chronically can't find motivation for it.
quotes.key_insight: Extracurricular contributions only count as signs of initiative when you're already meeting expectations on the core work you were hired to do — otherwise they become evidence of poor prioritization.

quotes.quote: When you are meeting expectations for your One Job — and you don't necessarily have to be dazzling, just competent and predictable — then picking up other work is a sign of initiative and investment. But when you aren't, you get no credit.

quotes.quote: You are all highly compensated subject matter experts who can find jobs relatively easily; there's no reason all the judgment and critique should flow in a single direction.
quotes.summary: The post provides a comprehensive guide of interview questions candidates should ask to evaluate whether a potential manager and management culture will be healthy or toxic. Charity argues that candidates should treat interviews as a two-way evaluation, offering specific questions for both managers and engineers that reveal red flags like involuntary managers, lack of career development paths, and evasive communication styles.
quotes.key_insight: Interviews are a two-way street, and candidates can reliably detect bad management culture by asking pointed questions about how work gets allocated, whether managers can say no directly, and whether management is treated as a peer role rather than a promotion.

quotes.quote: Most of all, can you get the manager to tell you 'no' on a thing you clearly want during the interview? Anyone who will look you straight in the eye and say 'no, and here's why', is someone you can probably trust to be straight with you down the line, in good times and bad.

quotes.quote: Most painful of all are the teams who have done all the hard parts — wired up continuous integration, achieved test coverage, etc — but still deploy by hand, thus depriving themselves of the payoff for their hard work.
quotes.summary: Teams that fail to implement true continuous deployment — where every merge auto-deploys to production in under 15 minutes — pay an enormous hidden cost in engineering headcount and organizational complexity. The delay between writing code and shipping it creates compounding pathologies that force teams to grow far larger than necessary to accomplish the same work.
quotes.key_insight: Deploy delays don't just slow you down linearly — they create compounding organizational pathologies that force exponential growth in headcount and complexity to accomplish the same work a small team with continuous deployment could handle.

quotes.quote: Your career is the single most valuable asset you have — it's a multimillion dollar appreciating asset, and you should curate and guide it with an eye toward longevity.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that early-career engineers should prioritize experience and career growth over salary optimization. She pushes back against the common recruiter advice to never go backward on compensation, framing it as short-sighted thinking that treats salary as a one-way escalator rather than a tool for building long-term career value.
quotes.key_insight: Early-career engineers should treat compensation as a tool for career optionality rather than a number that must always go up, because optimizing for salary over experience in your first decade can trap you in roles that stunt long-term growth.

quotes.quote: Consider it an investment in your long term career. Think of the extra money your company is paying you now as a hostage premium on top of your real market value.

quotes.quote: You can't recruit a Starbucks manager to manage a software engineering team any more than you would recruit an NFL coach to coach your Olympic gymnastics team. If you want to coach a team, you gotta know the game.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that engineering managers must have solid technical foundations, not just management skills, because modern engineering work involves complex sociotechnical systems where social and technical problems are inseparable. While non-technical managers can occasionally succeed through either exceptional personal qualities or organizational structures that compensate for their gaps, these are exceptions that shouldn't define hiring strategy.
quotes.key_insight: Engineering managers need both technical and social skills because modern software teams operate within complex sociotechnical systems where separating those domains creates fragile organizational structures and muddied accountability.

quotes.quote: These days we all work on, and from within, these incredibly complex sociotechnical systems and feedback loops that are so complex, there is rarely any problem that is purely 'social', 'tools', or 'technology'. It is all entwined and interconnected.

quotes.quote: As an engineer, you get that very tactile sense of merging code, solving puzzles and incrementally pushing the business forward. It has a rhythm and a powerful drip, drip, drip of dopamine, and as a manager you will never ever feel that.
quotes.summary: The post addresses the common struggle managers face in feeling worthwhile when they're no longer the ones building things. Charity argues that managerial satisfaction is fundamentally different from engineering satisfaction — it's slower, less visible, and lacks the dopamine drip of shipping code. She offers practical frameworks for locating your impact in big-picture outcomes, small human moments, and crisis situations, while calling out the performative selflessness managers are expected to display.
quotes.key_insight: Managerial worth is measured not in daily visible output but in the long trail of trust, team health, and crisis resilience you build over years — and pretending you don't need recognition for that work is dishonest.

quotes.quote: There is nothing wrong with wanting credit for your work, and affirmation and validation, and there is nothing inherently noble about not wanting those things.

quotes.quote: It's nigh impossible to have a high-performing team with a long lead time, and becomes drastically easier with a dramatically shorter lead time.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that CI/CD lead time—from code merge to production deploy—should be under 15 minutes, and that achieving this is central to building high-performing engineering teams. She provides a comprehensive, community-sourced catalog of anti-patterns and bottlenecks that slow down build pipelines, organized by category from test design to AWS infrastructure choices.
quotes.key_insight: A 15-minute CI/CD pipeline isn't a luxury—it's the foundation that aligns everything else for engineering team success, and most teams who think they can't achieve it are wrong.

quotes.quote: If you get CI/CD right, a lot of other critical functions, behaviors, and intuitions are aligned to be comfortably successful and correct with minimal effort. If you get it wrong, you will spend countless cycles chasing pathologies.

quotes.quote: There is a world of distance between being expert in *this system* and being an actual expert in your chosen craft. The second is seniority; the first is merely .. familiarity
quotes.summary: Charity Majors advises an engineer who became the most senior person on their team by default (at their first job) to leave and start over elsewhere. She argues that true seniority comes not from deep familiarity with one system, but from having learned and leveled up across multiple teams, stacks, and organizations over 5-8 years.
quotes.key_insight: True engineering seniority isn't earned by becoming the expert on one system — it's forged by starting over multiple times across different teams and stacks until you can succeed anywhere.

quotes.quote: Think of every job like an escalator — a 50-foot high escalator that takes about two years to ride to the top. But once you've summited, you stall out.

quotes.quote: It is engineering's responsibility to be on call and own their code. It is management's responsibility to make sure that on call does not suck. This is a handshake, it goes both ways, and if you do not hold up your end they should quit and leave you.
quotes.summary: On-call responsibility is a two-way compact: engineers should own their code in production, but management must ensure on-call doesn't suck by investing in tooling, tracking alerts, protecting on-call time from project work, and treating every out-of-hours page as urgent. Managers who fail to hold up their end of this bargain deserve to lose their engineers.
quotes.key_insight: On-call is a mutual compact where management owes engineers sustainable conditions — protected time, good tooling, alert tracking, and treating every page as urgent — and failing this obligation justifies engineers walking out the door.

quotes.quote: Tossing it off to ops after tests pass is nothing but a thinly veiled form of engineering classism, and you can't build high-performing systems by breaking up your feedback loops this way.

quotes.quote: Not every opportunity exists at every company at every time.
quotes.summary: Engineers frustrated about slow career progression often overlook that promotion opportunities are situational — not every company has the right openings, oxygen, or need at every level at every time. Title inflation from making exceptions undermines leveling systems, and engineers should strategically evaluate whether their current environment actually offers the growth opportunities they seek rather than passively waiting to be promoted.
quotes.key_insight: Career progression depends less on individual merit and more on whether your current environment has the structural oxygen — the right ratio of work, people, and need — to support your advancement.

quotes.quote: Any technical company should work hard to center and celebrate the work being done to build the product and make customers happy. Management is overhead, to be brutally frank about it, and we should not design organizations that would lead any rational, ambitious person to aspire to be overhead, should we?
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that the perception of management as a promotion is the root cause of bad managers persisting in tech, because returning to engineering is seen as a demotion that nobody wants to accept. She advocates for treating management as a lateral career change and support role, not a hierarchical step up, and provides a concrete roadmap for companies to restructure power dynamics so that engineering and management are seen as equally valuable but different paths.
quotes.key_insight: The cultural perception of management as a promotion traps bad managers in roles they shouldn't hold and starves organizations of great technical leaders who fear the stigma of "stepping down" — and companies must actively restructure power, pay, and prestige to break this cycle.

quotes.quote: That extra human layer is worse than useless; it is actively harmful. By insulating developers from the consequences of their actions, you are concealing from them the information they need to understand the consequences of their actions.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors tears apart the concept of centralized 'command center' war rooms for monitoring applications, arguing they are a waste of money rooted in outdated thinking. She identifies two fatal flaws: graphs can be automated and only catch known problems, and the people watching graphs aren't the ones who can actually fix issues. The approach actively harms engineering feedback loops by insulating developers from the consequences of their code.
quotes.key_insight: Centralized war rooms for monitoring are actively harmful because they break the essential feedback loop between developers and the production behavior of their code, and no amount of dashboard-watching can replace the contextual knowledge of the engineers who built the systems.

quotes.quote: The only people who can swiftly diagnose and fix complex systems issues are the people who build and maintain those systems, and those people are busy building and maintaining, not watching graphs.

quotes.quote: In my experience, most engineers become managers primarily due to organizational dysfunction. When you become a manager you acquire certain institutional powers, and you can use those powers to change the thing that makes you miserable.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that most engineers become managers to compensate for organizational dysfunction rather than the selfless reasons they claim in interviews. She contends that in healthy organizations, engineers shouldn't need to become managers for career progression, pay equity, or decision-making access, and that the only legitimate reason to try management is simple curiosity.
quotes.key_insight: Most engineers become managers to hack around organizational dysfunction, but in a healthy org the only honest reason to try management is simple curiosity — and the fastest way to cure the obsession with it is to actually do it.

quotes.quote: Like, if you could only deploy three end-to-end checks that would perform entire operations on your site and ensure they work at all times, what would they be?
quotes.summary: Charity Majors explains how to identify your team's critical path by asking 'what makes you money?' — the essential business operations that justify waking someone up at 3am. She argues teams should keep this list compact and explicit, which also clarifies that everything else can wait until morning.
quotes.key_insight: Your critical path is defined by asking 'what makes us money?' — keep the list of page-worthy operations as small as possible so you can explicitly acknowledge that everything else can wait.

quotes.quote: The idea here is to draw up a list of the things that are absolutely worth waking someone up to fix immediately, night or day, rain or shine. That list should be as compact and well-defined as possible.

quotes.quote: To the extent you can reduce a job to a set of metrics, that job can be automated away.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that individual engineering productivity cannot be meaningfully measured through metrics, as creative professional work is too complex to reduce to discrete measurements. She advocates instead for outcome-based management, team-level health metrics, well-calibrated managers with technical judgment, and DORA metrics as proxies for team effectiveness.
quotes.key_insight: Engineering productivity should be evaluated at the team level through outcomes and DORA metrics, not through individual metrics that incentivize gaming and ignore the most impactful work.

quotes.quote: Ethical problems are hard. Even the ones that seem straightforward on the face of them get stickier the closer you look at them. There are more stakeholders, more caveats, more cautionary tales, more unintended consequences than you can generally see at face value. It's like fractal hardness, and anyone who thinks it's easy is fooling themselves.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors describes Honeycomb's experiment of discussing hypothetical ethical dilemmas monthly as a team. She argues that ethical problems are fractally hard and that practicing these discussions when stakes are low helps teams make better decisions under pressure, builds trust, and amplifies employee voices.
quotes.key_insight: Regularly practicing ethical decision-making as a team when stakes are low builds the trust and judgment needed to navigate real crises well.

quotes.quote: People make the worst possible decisions when they're taken off guard, when they are in an unfamiliar situation (and often panicking). Talking through a bunch of nightmare scenarios is a way for us to exercise these decision-making muscles while the stakes are low.

quotes.quote: We succeed as a company not by doing everything on our list, but by saying no to the right things; by NOT-doing enough most things so we can focus on the few things we have identified that matter most.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors shares the advice Honeycomb gave employees during the pandemic: acknowledge that what's being asked is literally impossible, shed everything except your top priority, and spend zero energy on performing normalcy. The core message is that 30 minutes of focused creative energy on the biggest problem beats five hours of distracted availability theater.
quotes.key_insight: In crisis periods, leaders should explicitly give teams permission to shed work and stop performing normalcy, redirecting all available energy toward the single highest-impact problem.

quotes.quote: I would rather have 30 minutes of your creative energy applied to our biggest problem today than five hours of your distracted split-brain, juggling, trying to keep up with chat and seem like you're as available per usual today.

quotes.quote: If you kill one person, you're a murderer and we shut you out from society; kill ten and you are a monster; but if you kill thousands, or kill on behalf of the state, we give you medals and write books about you.
quotes.summary: Charity recommends Barbara Ehrenreich's 'Blood Rites' as quarantine reading, summarizing its thesis that humanity's sacred feelings toward war originate not from innate aggression but from our evolutionary history as prey animals who venerated blood sacrifice. The book argues we are the 'nouveaux riche of predators' who go to war because we were once prey, and this discomfort drives our ritualization of violence. She connects this framework to understanding cultural phenomena from horror movies to Tiger King.
quotes.key_insight: Ehrenreich's 'Blood Rites' argues that humanity sacralizes war not because we are natural predators but because we are former prey animals, and our ritualization of violence is a vestigial expression of ancient blood sacrifice born from that deep evolutionary insecurity.

quotes.quote: This is such a reliable bait and switch that any time you hear someone talking about 'metrics, logs and traces', you can be pretty damn sure there's no actual observability going on.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that observability is being deliberately watered down by monitoring vendors who rebrand their existing metrics, logs, and tracing products. True observability, borrowed from control theory, means the ability to ask any question about your system's internal state without predicting those questions in advance, which requires arbitrarily-wide structured events with high cardinality and high dimensionality — something no traditional monitoring tool provides.
quotes.key_insight: True observability is the ability to ask arbitrary questions about unknown system states without predicting them in advance, and vendors redefining it as 'metrics, logs, and traces' are actively preventing the industry from escaping its current dysfunction.

quotes.quote: You cannot have it both ways, where you block off a bunch of undeployable time AND you have robust, resilient, swift deploys.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that blocking Friday deploys is a symptom of unhealthy deployment practices, not a solution. Drawing on her experience at Parse and Honeycomb, she demonstrates that the real path to protecting engineers' work-life balance is building a tight build/ship/observe feedback loop with observability, single-changeset deploys, and observability-driven development culture.
quotes.key_insight: Blocking Friday deploys treats the symptom while worsening the disease — the real fix is investing in observability and tight deploy feedback loops so that every deploy, any day of the week, is safe and fast.

quotes.quote: SLOs are a powerful weapon to wield against micromanagers, meddlers, and feature-hungry PMs.
quotes.summary: SLOs serve as an abstraction layer that protects engineering teams from micromanagement, priority hijacking, and feature pressure by establishing clear boundaries and shared ownership of service quality. When stakeholders sign off on SLOs, they agree that reliability is the top priority, giving teams leverage to push back on disruptions and justify technical debt work. The framework works bidirectionally—teams that exceed their SLOs should stop polishing and ship features.
quotes.key_insight: SLOs are not primarily a technical tool for measuring uptime—they are an organizational contract that defines how other teams and managers are allowed to interact with your engineering team.

quotes.quote: Delete all your paging alerts, then page only on e2e alerts that mean users are in pain.
quotes.summary: Traditional monitoring accumulated excessive paging alerts because teams relied on pattern-matching from past failures to debug monolithic systems. With distributed systems, this approach collapses because failures are infinite and unique, so teams should delete all paging alerts and replace them with SLO-based alerts that only fire when users are actually impacted, using observability tools for debugging instead of alert clusters.
quotes.key_insight: In the era of distributed systems and observability, paging alerts should be radically reduced to only user-impacting SLO violations, because the old model of accumulating per-failure alerts was really a debugging crutch that observability tools now replace.

Rather than accepting deploy fear as normal, teams should invest in CI/CD pipelines, observability tooling, and smaller deployments to make shipping code a non-event. Slowing down deploys actually worsens reliability by batching changes and degrading engineers' judgment.
quotes.key_insight: Friday deploy freezes treat the symptom instead of the disease — teams should invest in fast, small, observable deployments rather than accepting fear of shipping as normal.

quotes.quote: Pain is a terrific teacher, but pain is an acute response. Chronic pain will hijack your reward pathways, your perspective, your relationships, and every other productive system and leave them stunted.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors reflects on her relationship with pain as a career growth strategy, from brute-forcing her way past public speaking terror to her painful tenure as CEO of Honeycomb. She concludes that while leaning into acute pain can build skills, chronic pain in a role you hate will only diminish you—sometimes the only way to succeed is to redefine success.
quotes.key_insight: Leaning into acute pain builds skills, but chronic pain in a misaligned role will destroy your capacity—knowing which battles to power through and which to walk away from is the defining career wisdom.

quotes.quote: Your only view of the system is through the instrumentation of your code.
quotes.summary: Traditional logging best practices were designed for monolithic systems with expensive disks and simple architectures, but they fail completely in distributed systems where investigation requires computation across many services. Engineers need to replace log lines and log levels with arbitrarily wide structured events — one per request per service — that capture rich context and feed into systems capable of ad hoc querying.
quotes.key_insight: Legacy logging best practices actively harm observability in distributed systems — engineers must replace scattered log lines with wide structured events that capture full request context, enabling the computational investigation that modern systems demand.

quotes.quote: Management is a career change, not a promotion, and nobody is very good at it when they first start.
quotes.summary: Engineering management is a career change, not a promotion, and managers face a critical fork: stay technical as a line manager (practicing the engineer/manager pendulum) or climb the organizational ladder—a mostly one-way trip that progressively erodes technical skills and narrows career options. New managers should commit to a minimum two-year tour of duty, resist the advice to completely stop coding, and understand that their technical skills begin seriously deteriorating after 3-5 years away from hands-on work.
quotes.key_insight: Engineering managers must consciously resist the default ladder-climbing narrative and periodically return to hands-on technical work, because the intersection of technical and people skills is what makes them effective—and that intersection has an expiration date.

quotes.quote: There actually seems to be a direct link between teams that give engineers lots of leeway to own their technical decisions and that team's ability to hire and retain top-tier talent, particularly senior talent.
quotes.summary: Engineering teams that give developers broad technical autonomy often end up with unsustainable software sprawl across languages, databases, and tools. The solution is creating a 'Golden Path' of fully-supported default components chosen by a small council of senior engineers, where developers can still choose non-standard tools but must support them entirely on their own. This preserves developer agency while creating natural pressure toward convergence and operability.
quotes.key_insight: The best way to reverse software sprawl without killing developer autonomy is to make the standardized path so well-supported that choosing anything else carries a real personal cost.

quotes.summary: Charity Majors distills hard-won lessons from building and running platforms at scale into ten commandments, emphasizing the need to protect the platform from individual users, center observability on user experience rather than system health, and invest in fine-grained operational controls. The advice draws on her experience at Parse and Honeycomb, focusing on what it takes to keep a multi-tenant platform alive and healthy.
quotes.key_insight: Running a platform at scale requires ruthless prioritization, user-centered observability, and the discipline to protect the platform from individual users while giving every user the tools to help themselves.

quotes.quote: There's only one thing that has reliably accelerated my development as a leader or manager, and that is forming bonds and swapping stories with my peers.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that the most effective way to develop leadership skills is not through books, coaches, or mentors, but through peer relationships where leaders swap stories and support each other. She describes how she built small peer groups that met regularly to practice vulnerability and share real, contextual advice, and publishes her framework for others to replicate.
quotes.key_insight: Leadership development is best accelerated not through formal training or hierarchical mentorship, but through reciprocal peer relationships built on vulnerability, trust, and the exchange of real, contextual stories.

quotes.quote: Deploy software is the most important software you have.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that shipping software should not be a scary, high-risk event, and that organizations must invest in making their deploy pipelines resilient to failure rather than trying to eliminate errors entirely. She advocates for software ownership where every engineer who writes code also deploys, monitors, and debugs it in production, creating shorter feedback loops and better software. The path forward involves treating deploy tooling as first-class infrastructure, embracing failure as normal, and incrementally gaining confidence in releases through canaries, feature flags, and observability.
quotes.key_insight: Making deploys safe isn't about preventing all failures — it's about building resilient pipelines, embracing software ownership, and creating a culture where failures are small, frequent, non-events that everyone knows how to detect and recover from.

quotes.quote: Managing stateful services like databases means that you need to be more paranoid than you did with stateless services.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors dissects a MongoDB outage caused by CleverTap's reckless storage engine migration, arguing that the real problem wasn't MongoDB but engineers treating stateful database services with the same cavalier attitude they use for stateless services. She emphasizes that managing databases requires paranoia, careful planning, and months of testing—not weekend upgrades—and urges 'accidental DBAs' to invest in learning proper data management practices.
quotes.key_insight: Database operations demand paranoia and patience—treating stateful services with the same move-fast-and-break-things attitude used for stateless services is a recipe for catastrophic, often unrecoverable data loss.
