quotes.quote: A lot of the philosophical volleying between devops / SRE comes down to a failure to recognize the overwhelming power of context.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors argues that the DevOps vs SRE debate is largely a failure to appreciate context — Google's SRE practices are excellent for Google but shouldn't be blindly applied elsewhere. She contends that DevOps philosophy is more adaptable and crowd-sourced than Google's prescriptive SRE model, and criticizes Google's insularity and apparent unawareness of the broader industry's decade of cultural transformation around operations.
quotes.key_insight: The DevOps vs SRE debate is fundamentally a context problem — Google's SRE practices are contextually brilliant but the industry needs the more adaptable, crowd-sourced DevOps philosophy because one-size-fits-all solutions don't exist.

You still own reliability, debuggability, and storage even when you don't run the infrastructure, so application engineers must develop operations-minded thinking about their core differentiators.
quotes.key_insight: Serverless and SaaS abstractions are inevitable and largely positive, but they shift — not eliminate — operational responsibility, requiring application engineers to think deeply about reliability, debuggability, and their core differentiators.

quotes.quote: Since you have no ability to debug AWS networking via normal linux utilities, my best debugging tip for VPC networking is still, when you get really stuck open up all the SG ports and see if that fixes it.
quotes.summary: A practical brain dump of hard-won Terraform and AWS networking tips covering security groups, VPC peering, NAT gateways, route tables, and other infrastructure-as-code gotchas. Charity shares specific configurations and debugging strategies learned through painful trial and error, emphasizing the unintuitive nature of AWS networking when managed through Terraform.
quotes.key_insight: AWS networking via Terraform is full of unintuitive gotchas that aren't documented well, but once you've fought through the pain the payoff of reproducible infrastructure is enormous.

quotes.quote: BUT MIND THE FUCKING TRAP. You do not attach these NAT gateways to your PRIVATE subnets, you attach them to the PUBLIC FUCKING SUBNETS, and then a route from the private subnet to that gateway.

quotes.quote: If you can't safely test your changes in isolation away from prod, you don't have infrastructure as code.
quotes.summary: After a catastrophic Terraform outage caused by a poisoned shared state file — triggered by simple staging changes that took down all environments — Charity Majors walks through the painful debugging and recovery process, then argues forcefully for using separate Terraform state files per environment to limit blast radius.
quotes.key_insight: Sharing a single Terraform state file across environments means any change to staging can destroy production — separate state files per environment are the minimum viable safety net for infrastructure as code.

quotes.quote: If you can't spin up a full copy of your infra and test it, you don't actually have 'infrastructure as code.' You just have … some code, and duct tape.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors compares three AWS networking patterns for managing multiple environments (flat network, account-per-env, VPC-per-env) and argues strongly for the VPC-per-environment model. She debunks outdated beliefs about VPC limitations and makes the case that VPCs enable treating infrastructure as testable, promotable code rather than duct-taped configurations.
quotes.key_insight: VPC-per-environment is the best AWS networking pattern because it gives you real isolation while enabling you to treat infrastructure as testable, promotable code — which is the only way 'infrastructure as code' actually means anything.

quotes.quote: VPC is the future and it is awesome, and unless you have some VERY SPECIFIC AND CONVINCING reasons to do otherwise, you should be spinning up a VPC per environment with orchestration and prob doing it from CI on every code commit, almost like it's just like, you know, code.

quotes.quote: Because I am fucking sick and tired of not having versioned infrastructure. Jesus christ, the ways my teams have bent over backwards to fake infra versioning after the fact.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors shares her brutally honest experience adopting Terraform for infrastructure orchestration, documenting the progression from initial delight through debugging hell with modules and inscrutable error messages. She concludes that Terraform is best-in-class for infrastructure orchestration but is still too immature for high-reliability production use, recommending it only for defining static infrastructure bones rather than managing dynamic resources.
quotes.key_insight: Terraform is the best available option for versioned infrastructure orchestration, but its immaturity means you should constrain it to static infrastructure bones and never let it manage dynamic resources in high-reliability production environments.

quotes.quote: There is precious little evidence that TF was developed by or for anyone with experience running production systems at scale, but it's definitely not as actively hostile as CloudFormation, so it's got that going for it.

quotes.quote: Trauma is the norm. Acquisitions are always massively disruptive and upsetting, even the good ones.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors reflects on the emotional and professional reality of being acquired by a large company (Facebook's acquisition of Parse), offering hard-won advice for startup founders and engineers going through the same experience. She emphasizes that trauma is normal, your product and customers are no longer yours, and you must either adapt to the new corporate culture or leave.
quotes.key_insight: Surviving an acquisition means accepting that your product, customers, and culture now belong to someone else — and deciding honestly whether you can live with that or need to walk away.

quotes.quote: Being a manager at a big company makes this depressing Swiss cheese out of your calendar. You can't really carve out the heads-down time you would need in order to do challenging technical work.
quotes.summary: Charity Majors recaps her 2015 highlights: speaking at 21 conferences while restless as a manager, championing MongoDB's maturation through the RocksDB storage engine integration at Parse, and leaving Parse/Facebook after 3.5 years when comfort became stagnation.
quotes.key_insight: Growth requires leaving comfort behind — whether that means a database outgrowing its storage engine or an engineer outgrowing a role that no longer challenges them.
